imageCaption: A vintage radio dial glows with overlapping voices, showing both the frequencies they're hearing and the frequencies that haven't happened yet, all occupying the same space
text:
The radio started speaking in Aaliyah's voice.

She was in the communications cabin, cross-referencing her phone's glitch database with the mysterious document her future self had apparently already written, when the ship's old radio—the one that usually just pulled in crackling weather reports and the occasional transmission from other vessels—began broadcasting something that hadn't happened yet.

"—can't explain how the resonance works to someone who isn't living inside it," future-Aaliyah was saying, her voice a few years older and significantly more tired. "You have to experience the way your family's chaos creates an anchor point. You have to see the moment when spontaneity becomes the most rigid, predictable force in the universe."

Aaliyah stopped writing. Her hands had frozen mid-motion, which was probably the correct response to hearing yourself give an explanation you hadn't actually prepared yet.

"River," she said carefully, "am I hearing tomorrow's radio conversations or am I hearing... what?"

The compass trembled. "The broadcast bleed," River said. "The closer you get to Curaçao, the more the glitches are pulling information backward and forward simultaneously. The radio is picking up transmissions that exist in multiple timeframes at once. It's not just hearing tomorrow. It's hearing tomorrow speaking about yesterday while you're living today."

This, Aaliyah decided, was arguably the strangest glitch yet.

She climbed down from the communications cabin to find her entire family gathered in the galley, staring at the radio like it had personally betrayed them. Which, technically, it had—by revealing secrets that hadn't been discovered yet.

"This is the creepiest glitch," Elijah announced. He was holding his journal, ready to document the event with the gravity it probably deserved. "Yesterday's glitch was just flowers. Tomorrow's glitch is probably going to be worse. But this glitch is weird because it's rude."

"It's talking about things we haven't figured out yet," Luisana added. She was on her second coffee, which meant she'd already processed the existential implications and was simply operating on caffeine and journalistic instinct. "It's like reading someone's diary before they've lived the days they're describing."

The radio crackled. Another voice emerged—this one definitely not-Aaliyah, but distinctly young. Elijah at maybe nineteen, more confident than he should be, explaining something about probability with the authority of someone who'd figured it out and refused to let uncertainty complicate his answer.

"—the rocks will tell you where the island wants you," future-Elijah was saying, "if you're willing to believe that rocks can have preferences. And we are. That's our whole thing—believing things that don't make logical sense."

Anthony had gone very quiet in the way that meant he was processing multiple layers of reality at once. He was holding his pegboard of license plates, and Aaliyah could see that one plate in particular was glowing—the one from Curaçao, which shouldn't exist yet because they hadn't actually arrived. But it was there, vivid and real, a promise from a future that was apparently broadcasting backward through a very confused radio system.

"Can we turn it off?" Aaliyah asked, which was probably the least useful question in the history of questions, given that this was a temporal glitch and turning off a radio had never solved anything.

"I wouldn't recommend it," River said. "The broadcast bleed is showing you the moments you'll need to remember. The radio is selecting which conversations to pull from tomorrow specifically because they're answers to questions you don't know you're asking yet."

The radio static shifted. A new voice—Luisana, older, calmer, the way she'd sound after three more days of approaching an island she'd secretly wanted for years but was too practical to admit wanting.

"—found the yellow door," future-Luisana was saying. "It's exactly the color I imagined, except somehow more true. And Anthony had the license plate from Curaçao hanging in his pegboard the entire time we were traveling, which means some part of him always knew where we were going, even when he was still making spontaneous decisions about flight paths."

Sully had positioned himself in front of the radio with the intensity of a dog who was taking his oracle duties seriously. He barked—one long bark, the serious kind, the kind that meant a major temporal situation was occurring—and the broadcast shifted again.

This time, it was all three children speaking simultaneously, their voices layered like a chord being played across time: Aaliyah explaining the resonance frequency, Elijah revealing the probability calculations of rocks, and a third voice—slightly foreign, not-yet-familiar, but definitely still family. Singing in Spanish, the way someone would sing who'd been practicing for a future that didn't know they were practicing yet.

"Is that—" Anthony started.

"Someone who should have boarded this boat three days ago," River said quietly. "Someone whose probability has been traveling alongside your route, waiting for the moment when the broadcast bleed would confirm that she was always supposed to be here."

The radio went static. Then clear. Then static again. Like it was trying to find the right frequency and discovering that "the right frequency" was actually all of them at once, all the timelines speaking through the same speaker, all the possible Gomezes confirming that yes, they were arriving at an island, and yes, the island had been expecting them, and yes, the spontaneous family was about to discover that their chaos had been building toward something structured and beautiful the entire time.

Aaliyah looked at her family's faces—confusion and wonder and the beginning of understanding that tomorrow was trying very hard to tell them something important.

"Three days," she heard herself say, though she wasn't sure if it was her voice or future-Aaliyah's voice, or if maybe in a glitch like this, those were the same thing entirely.

"Three days," her father echoed, and he was holding his wife and his children and his collection of miniature license plates, and somewhere in Curaçao, a yellow door was beginning to prepare itself for the arrival of the family it had been waiting for all along.

Sully pressed his nose against the radio speaker like he was greeting someone he already knew, and the broadcast bleed finally, mercifully, faded into static—satisfied, it seemed, that the message had been delivered.

Tomorrow, the family would figure out what the radio had been trying to tell them.

Tonight, they simply sat together and listened to the shape of a future that was broadcasting backward, insisting that they arrive, insisting that they stay, insisting that yes, even a family as gloriously spontaneous as the Gomezes could come home.
